The Colts, mentioned in that test, were the now-defunct Baltimore Colts.
Nearly 23 years ago in the dark of a snowy night, the team's owner abruptly moved the Colts to Indianapolis.
Baltimore didn't get another NFL franchise until the Ravens came in 1996.
So today's match up between the Ravens and the Colts has special significance for longtime Baltimore fans like Barry Levinson.
He joins us on the phone now from his home in Connecticut.
Mr. Levinson, welcome to the program.
Thank you.
Hi.
ELLIOTT I take it sneaking the Colts away in the dead of night didn't go over so well in Baltimore back in 1984.
No, it didn't.
Not at all.
I mean, it was the most horrific way to lose a team.
To be sneaking out in the middle of the night with moving vans, was about, I guess, the lowest of the low.
And it made fans like you pretty ready to root against Indianapolis from then on.
Exactly.
I mean there was many factors that went into it.
A lot of people outside of Baltimore don't quite get the picture.
I mean the team was so, you know, part of the city and the fabric of the town.
When the Colts left, there was a Baltimore, you know, marching band and they continued to march in Baltimore without a team, every year.
For 12 years they continued to march.
I read that you actually started going to Baltimore Colts games the very first year that the city got its NFL franchise.
That was 1953?
That's right.
Now, the Ravens have been in Baltimore for about 10 years now.
Was it easy for you to get behind the Ravens and become a Ravens fan after being a Colts fan for much of your life?
Yes.
I think in the end that we all sort of embraced the Ravens right away because we got another football team and we were thrilled and the Ravens have been, you know, terrific in Baltimore.
They have great support.
It's the lingering thing that there are two - that there are two Colts.
And the injustice really comes to the surface when here you are tonight going to play.
Yeah, I mean it's very strange to suddenly, you know, to see the blue and white, the horseshoe, and they are now the team to root against.
You know, it does a real mind game on your head.
I heard that that test that the bride had to take at the diner was actually a tradition that comes from your own family?
I don't know that it was a tradition, but it came from a cousin of mine and he did want to give his wife a football test, you know, before he married her.
And he wanted to know how up on the Colts history she really was and what kind of fan she might be.
So that, in fact, is based on truth.
Has she proven to be a good Baltimore fan?
Well, I think they're dealing as best as they can.
But they feel, like us, overwhelmed by it.
And they rely on us to help them.
Remittances.

They want to believe that somebody who comes from fairly humble background, starting out life in an orphanage, can in fact, articulate a vision for this country that would be compelling, and be the next president of the United States.
That's what America is all about.
Well, Governor Vilsack, thank you very much for talking to us.
Thank you.
It's Tom Vilsack, the governor of Iowa, announced candidate for the Democratic presidential nomination, speaking to us today from Minneapolis.
It's called the REAL ID Act.
Congress is currently considering legislation that would prevent states from giving driver's licenses to undocumented immigrants.
The bill would also make it tougher to get asylum in the US.
Marianne McCune of member station WNYC has been following one asylum applicant from Sierra Leone, and she sent this report.
Bashir's(ph) life in New York City is a kind of purgatory between the bloody civil war he escaped in Sierra Leone and the safety he hopes to find in the United States.
Take care of the doors.
So when he watches the city go by through the windows of an elevated subway train, he's not all here.
My person is here.
(Soundbite of bell)
On my mind--in the trouble over there.
Six years ago in Sierra Leone, he says, he came home to find a group of rebels about to rape his sister.
His father was a local imam, and when he tried to stop them, Bashir says, they shot his father.
I see--kill my father, like that.
Bashir's wife had already fled the civil war with their children.
Now he escaped, and eventually friends helped him come to the US to apply for asylum.
Pangaj Malak(ph) is his attorney.
He would get very emotional and very upset.
It was very devastating for him to even talk about it.
But Malak will have to push Bashir to tell his story again and again, with as much specific detail and consistency as possible.
In the immigration system, applicants must prove they're telling the truth, that they have a legitimate fear of persecution.
Asylum officers and government attorneys and judges scour the stories for holes.
They have to be that way because there are so many cases that are not true.
Of the tens of thousands who tried to apply for asylum last year, some 40,000 got their cases heard.
And of that group, about a third were granted the right to stay.
But people who are rejected by immigration judges can appeal their cases to a federal court.
And, say proponents of tightening laws, many use appeals to delay deportation.
The REAL ID Act would give more weight to the decisions of immigration judges who could reject applicants based on any inconsistency in their story or a suspicious-seeming demeanor.
Rosemary Jenks of the group Numbers USA says that authority will allow them to prevent people from manipulating the system.
This bill is specifically trying to ensure that we're not going to give asylum to a terrorist and that we're not going to let a terrorist use the asylum system in order to prolong his stay.
But opponents of the measure predict people facing horrific situations at home will be turned away from the US with no chance to appeal.
And Anwen Hughes of the advocacy group Human Rights First says since September 11th, applying for asylum has already gotten harder.
The level of corroboration that's expected has been steadily rising.
The level of investigation of people has been increasing.
Hughes says it's difficult now to get enough documents to prove who applicants are and what they saw.
She fears obtaining all the evidence REAL ID would require will prove near impossible in cases where applicants fled great danger or persecution and brought nothing with them.
This my last work.
